Scenario.title: APT28 Nearest Neighbor Campaign: Healthcare Infrastructure Compromise
Scenario.overview: Healthcare and Public Health Complex [A sophisticated APT28 campaign leverages multi-stage attacks to infiltrate healthcare networks, exploiting vulnerabilities in medical devices and IT systems to exfiltrate sensitive patient data and disrupt operations.]
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Public Information Source, Process, Internet-facing assets, Target System Artifact, Command, Application Log, OS Process, File, Domain Name Registrar
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT) Gathering, Process Creation (Mechanism), General Access Method, Living-Off-The-Land Binaries (LOLBINS), Command Execution (Mechanism), Application Log Content (Mechanism), Process Injection, Reg.exe Utility, Pass the Hash, File Modification (Mechanism), Process Creation (Mechanism), IP ID Sequencing Probe, File Access (Mechanism)

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The attackers gather information on healthcare infrastructure, employee roles, and vulnerabilities through public sources such as social media, job postings, and vendor websites to identify potential entry points.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC-670: Software Development Tools Maliciously Altered
stage.description: The threat actors develop custom payloads targeting medical devices or hospital management systems by compromising software deployment tools, ensuring compatibility with specific healthcare IT environments.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: The attackers send spear-phished emails impersonating trusted vendors or internal departments, embedding malicious attachments that exploit human error to gain initial foothold in the network.

stage.name: Execution
stage.description: The malware executes using legitimate tools like PowerShell or Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to avoid detection while maintaining operational security.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: The attackers modify system startup entries (plist files) to ensure persistence, allowing the malware to execute automatically upon system reboot.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: The adversaries leverage default credentials or misconfigured remote services in healthcare IT systems to escalate privileges and gain administrative control over critical infrastructure.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: The malware injects malicious code into legitimate processes to hide its activities from security monitoring tools and evade detection mechanisms.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC-670: Software Development Tools Maliciously Altered
stage.description: The attackers steal login credentials from infected endpoints by exploiting compromised software deployment tools during lateral movement phases.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: The threat actors perform network sniffing using utilities like Reg.exe to map internal network topology and identify critical systems such as PACS/EMR servers.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC-461: Web Services API Signature Forgery Leveraging Hash Function Extension Weakness
stage.description: The attackers move laterally across the network using stolen credentials to compromise additional systems and expand their control over the healthcare infrastructure.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC-675: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: The malware aggregates sensitive patient data or research findings from compromised systems by corrupting or deleting critical files to maximize impact.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: The attackers use encrypted DNS queries to stealthily exfiltrate data out of the network, masking malicious traffic as normal network activity.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: The final phase corrupts or encrypts essential healthcare services by accessing and modifying critical files, causing operational downtime and disrupting patient care.

Scenario.summary: [This complex APT28 campaign demonstrates advanced techniques to infiltrate healthcare networks, emphasizing the need for robust endpoint protection, credential management, and continuous monitoring to mitigate such threats.]
